_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AAN/EFNS 2008 (Cruccu PMID 18717729), ICHD-3 (Headache Classification Committee 2018 PMID 29368949), Maarbjerg Lancet Neurology 2017, Bendtsen J Headache Pain 2019 review, and Tatli Acta Neurochirurgica MVD literature. Dosing checked against UpToDate Lexicomp; surgical outcomes against Sindou and Barker MVD long-term series.

h1Title: Trigeminal Neuralgia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Trigeminal neuralgia is a facial pain disorder defined by brief paroxysms of electric-shock-like pain in the territory of the trigeminal nerve, usually triggered by light touch, chewing, brushing the teeth, or a breeze across the cheek. Incidence runs at roughly 12-27 new cases per 100,000 people each year, with women affected 1.7 times more often than men and peak onset between ages 50 and 70. Attacks last from a fraction of a second to two minutes, strike one side of the face almost always in the V2 (maxillary) or V3 (mandibular) branches, and are followed by a brief refractory period. The pain is among the most severe known to medicine, and untreated patients face substantial weight loss, depression, and suicide risk. Carbamazepine remains first-line drug therapy, and microvascular decompression offers durable relief in classical neurovascular-compression cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence 12-27 per 100,000 per year; lifetime prevalence approximately 0.16-0.3%
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio 1.7:1; right side affected slightly more often than left; bilateral involvement under 5% and raises suspicion of multiple sclerosis
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset age 50-70; under-40 onset, especially bilateral, suggests secondary cause such as MS
keyStats.globalCases: Roughly 2-3% of multiple sclerosis patients develop trigeminal neuralgia, often younger and bilaterally

definition: Trigeminal neuralgia (ICD-10: G50.0) is a paroxysmal facial neuropathic pain syndrome arising from dysfunction of the fifth cranial nerve. The International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition (ICHD-3) defines it as recurrent unilateral brief electric-shock-like pain abrupt in onset and termination, limited to the distribution of one or more divisions of the trigeminal nerve, and triggered by innocuous stimuli. ICHD-3 separates the condition into three forms: classical trigeminal neuralgia, caused by morphological changes in the trigeminal nerve root from neurovascular compression at the root entry zone (most often by the superior cerebellar artery); secondary trigeminal neuralgia, caused by an identifiable underlying disorder such as a multiple sclerosis plaque, vestibular schwannoma, epidermoid cyst, or arteriovenous malformation; and idiopathic trigeminal neuralgia, where no compression or lesion is found on high-resolution MRI. A subset of patients also experiences continuous background facial pain between paroxysms, formerly called atypical or type 2 trigeminal neuralgia and now described in ICHD-3 as trigeminal neuralgia with concomitant continuous pain. The condition is managed primarily by neurologists, with neurosurgery, pain medicine, and oral-facial specialists involved when refractory or surgical management is needed.

typesClassification.description: Caused by demonstrable neurovascular compression with morphological change (atrophy or displacement) of the trigeminal nerve root at the brainstem entry zone, typically by an arterial loop of the superior cerebellar or anterior inferior cerebellar artery. Accounts for roughly 75% of cases.

Attributed to an identifiable underlying disorder — multiple sclerosis plaque in the pontine trigeminal pathway, cerebellopontine angle tumor (vestibular schwannoma, meningioma, epidermoid cyst), arteriovenous malformation, or basilar dolichoectasia. Accounts for approximately 15% of cases.
No neurovascular compression with morphological change and no underlying disorder identified on high-quality MRI. Accounts for around 10% of cases.

typesClassification.type: Trigeminal neuralgia with concomitant continuous pain
typesClassification.description: Paroxysmal attacks plus a background of continuous or near-continuous moderate facial pain between attacks. Responds less reliably to drug therapy and to surgery than purely paroxysmal disease.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, severe, unilateral facial pain described as electric-shock-like, stabbing, or lancinating, lasting from a fraction of a second up to two minutes per paroxysm.
Pain limited to one or more divisions of the trigeminal nerve, most commonly the maxillary (V2) and mandibular (V3) branches; isolated ophthalmic (V1) involvement is seen in fewer than 5% of cases.
Trigger-evoked attacks brought on by innocuous stimuli — light touch to the face, chewing, brushing the teeth, shaving, washing the face, talking, smiling, or a draft of cool air across the cheek.
Discrete trigger zones, usually small areas around the nasolabial fold, upper lip, or chin, where minimal contact reliably provokes a paroxysm.
A brief refractory period of seconds to minutes after a severe attack during which the same trigger fails to produce pain.
Clusters of attacks lasting hours, separated by pain-free intervals of days, weeks, or months — the natural history is often relapsing and remitting in early disease.
Preserved sensation and normal neurological examination between attacks in classical and idiopathic forms; persistent sensory loss raises suspicion of a secondary cause.
Weight loss, dehydration, and avoidance of eating, drinking, talking, or oral hygiene because of fear that these activities will provoke an attack.
Depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation related to severe, unpredictable pain — the disorder has historically been called the suicide disease for this reason.
Continuous low-grade aching or burning background pain between paroxysms in patients with the concomitant-continuous-pain subtype.
earlyWarningSigns: Brief, fleeting jabs of facial pain triggered by light touch, often initially mistaken for a dental problem
Pain that comes and goes in clusters with pain-free remissions of weeks or months — a relapsing pattern early in the disease
Increasing avoidance of cold wind, shaving, or specific foods on one side of the face
Repeated dental procedures (root canals, extractions) that fail to relieve facial pain — a frequent prelude to diagnosis
Unexplained weight loss combined with new unilateral facial pain in an adult over 50
emergencySigns: Bilateral trigeminal pain, especially in a patient under 50 — urgent MRI is needed to exclude multiple sclerosis or a structural lesion
Persistent sensory loss, jaw weakness, hearing change, or other cranial nerve findings — suggests a secondary cause such as a cerebellopontine angle tumor
Suicidal thoughts or active plans related to uncontrolled pain — requires urgent psychiatric and pain-management input
Fever, neck stiffness, or rash in a patient with new facial pain — exclude herpes zoster ophthalmicus or other infectious cause

Severe drug reaction within the first 8 weeks of carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine — rash, mucosal involvement, eosinophilia, hyponatremia under 125 mmol/L — requires immediate discontinuation and assessment

causes.cause: Neurovascular compression at the trigeminal root entry zone
causes.description: An artery (most often the superior cerebellar artery, less often the anterior inferior cerebellar artery or a vein) pulses against the nerve root where it leaves the pons. Over years this produces focal demyelination and ectopic spontaneous discharges in adjacent fibers, generating the paroxysmal pain. This is the dominant cause in classical trigeminal neuralgia.

causes.cause: Multiple sclerosis plaque in the central trigeminal pathway
causes.description: A demyelinating lesion in the pontine root entry zone disrupts conduction in the trigeminal sensory tract. Trigeminal neuralgia develops in roughly 2-5% of MS patients, often years before classical neuralgia would otherwise begin, frequently bilaterally, and at a younger age.

Vestibular schwannoma, meningioma, or epidermoid cyst can stretch or distort the trigeminal root. These tumors account for roughly 2-5% of trigeminal neuralgia cases; the pain may be the first symptom before cranial nerve deficits appear.

Vascular anomalies that produce sustained mechanical pressure on the nerve. Basilar dolichoectasia — an elongated, tortuous basilar artery — is the most common vascular cause beyond simple loops of the superior cerebellar artery.

Injury to peripheral branches of the trigeminal nerve during dental procedures, maxillofacial surgery, or facial trauma can produce neuropathic pain that resembles trigeminal neuralgia. ICHD-3 classifies this as painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathy, a separate diagnosis, but presentations can overlap.

Familial clustering is reported in roughly 1-2% of cases, with autosomal dominant inheritance described in a small number of pedigrees. Genome-wide studies have not identified a single dominant locus, but ion-channel variants are under active investigation.

riskFactors.description: Incidence rises sharply after age 50, peaking between 60 and 70, as the vertebrobasilar arterial tree becomes more tortuous and more likely to compress the trigeminal root.
Women are affected 1.7 times more often than men; the reasons are not fully explained but may relate to differences in cerebellopontine angle anatomy and longer life expectancy.
MS patients have a 20-fold increased risk of trigeminal neuralgia compared with the general population; roughly 2-5% of MS patients develop it during their illness.

Long-standing hypertension contributes to arterial elongation and tortuosity, increasing the likelihood of neurovascular contact with the trigeminal root. The association is most consistent in older women.
A first-degree relative with the condition raises personal risk modestly. Familial cases tend to present earlier and more often bilaterally.
Significant facial trauma, dental implant placement, or third-molar extraction can precipitate trigeminal neuropathic pain, sometimes mimicking trigeminal neuralgia.
Schwannomas, meningiomas, epidermoid cysts, and arteriovenous malformations all elevate risk by deforming the trigeminal root.

affectedDemographics: Incidence highest in women aged 60-69 (approximately 25 per 100,000 person-years)
Bilateral disease is rare overall (<5%) but reaches 14% in multiple sclerosis-associated trigeminal neuralgia
Right side affected in roughly 60% of unilateral cases — likely an anatomical artifact of vertebrobasilar geometry
Pediatric trigeminal neuralgia is exceptional and should prompt a search for an underlying structural lesion
Cohort studies (Maarbjerg 2015, Denmark) report a delay from first symptom to diagnosis of 6-10 years on average, reflecting frequent initial misdiagnosis as dental pain
diagnosisOverview: Trigeminal neuralgia is a clinical diagnosis built on the ICHD-3 criteria: recurrent unilateral brief paroxysms of electric-shock-like facial pain in trigeminal distribution, triggered by innocuous stimuli, with no clinically evident neurological deficit between attacks. A careful history that establishes the lancinating quality, paroxysmal time course, trigger sensitivity, refractory period, and exact branch distribution is more diagnostic than any test. Examination should look for sensory loss in V1, V2, or V3, jaw weakness, corneal reflex change, and other cranial nerve findings — any of which raises suspicion of a secondary cause. Dental and sinus pathology must be excluded clinically; a dental opinion is often part of the workup because patients frequently present after multiple unsuccessful dental procedures. High-resolution MRI of the brain with dedicated trigeminal nerve sequences is mandatory in all patients, both to identify neurovascular compression with morphological change (supporting a classical diagnosis and informing surgical planning) and to exclude multiple sclerosis plaques and cerebellopontine angle tumors. Constructive interference in steady state (CISS) and fast imaging employing steady-state acquisition (FIESTA) sequences depict the trigeminal nerve, surrounding cisternal vessels, and any compressing vascular loop with high spatial resolution. Time-of-flight MR angiography adds vascular detail. The AAN/EFNS 2008 guideline recommends routine MRI for every patient with trigeminal neuralgia regardless of clinical confidence in a classical diagnosis. Electrophysiological testing, including trigeminal reflex studies, can support diagnosis in atypical cases but is not required for routine practice.

diagnosticTests.test: Detailed clinical history and ICHD-3 criteria
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the diagnosis. Key features are unilateral pain limited to trigeminal distribution, attacks lasting seconds to two minutes, electric-shock or stabbing quality, trigger-evoked attacks from innocuous stimuli, refractory period after severe attacks, and absence of clinically evident neurological deficit.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A neurologist or pain physician asks about the location, quality, duration, frequency, triggers, and response to prior treatment. A pain diary across 1-2 weeks is helpful. The consultation typically lasts 30-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies cranial nerve deficits that suggest a secondary cause. Specific attention to facial sensation in all three trigeminal divisions, corneal reflex, jaw strength and symmetry, and adjacent cranial nerves (VII, VIII).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless bedside testing — light touch and pinprick to each trigeminal division, jaw clench, smile, hearing, and balance assessment. Takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with dedicated trigeminal nerve sequences (CISS/FIESTA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory in every patient. Identifies neurovascular compression with morphological change of the trigeminal root, multiple sclerosis plaques, cerebellopontine angle tumors, and vascular malformations. The AAN/EFNS 2008 guideline (Cruccu et al) recommends MRI as the only routine investigation in newly diagnosed disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan in a closed magnet. CISS or FIESTA thin-section heavily T2-weighted sequences are added to the standard brain protocol; contrast is given if a tumor is suspected. No radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates the vascular anatomy around the trigeminal root entry zone in detail. Useful when planning microvascular decompression to identify the offending vessel and any accessory loops.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Added to the same MRI session; no separate appointment or radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: Trigeminal reflex testing (blink reflex, masseter inhibitory reflex)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Optional electrophysiological investigation. Used in research and in atypical cases to confirm dysfunction of the trigeminal sensory pathway and to support a diagnosis of symptomatic trigeminal neuralgia when imaging is unclear.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes record reflex responses to brief electrical stimulation of the trigeminal nerve branches. The study takes 30-60 minutes and produces mild discomfort during stimulation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes dental, periodontal, sinus, and temporomandibular joint pathology that mimics trigeminal neuralgia. Particularly valuable when the pain is restricted to V2 or V3 and the patient has had recent dental work.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dental examination plus a panoramic radiograph or cone-beam CT as needed. Coordinated with the neurology workup rather than performed in isolation.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests before starting carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Baseline complete blood count, liver function, sodium, and renal function. HLA-B*1502 screening is recommended in Han Chinese, Thai, and other Southeast Asian populations before carbamazepine because of a strong association with Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw with same-day results. HLA typing where indicated is a single test that does not need repeating.

treatmentOverview: Treatment runs along two stages — medical management first, then interventional procedures in patients who fail or cannot tolerate drug therapy. Carbamazepine is first-line and the only drug with Level A evidence from the AAN/EFNS 2008 guideline; starting dose is 100 mg twice daily titrated every 3-7 days to clinical effect, with most patients responding between 400 and 1200 mg per day in divided doses. Reported numbers needed to treat for pain relief range from 1.7 to 2.6, among the best in any neuropathic-pain indication. Oxcarbazepine 600-1800 mg per day is the principal alternative — better tolerated than carbamazepine for many patients, with similar efficacy though slightly weaker evidence. Lamotrigine, baclofen, gabapentin, and pregabalin are second-line agents used as add-ons or substitutes when sodium-channel blockade is poorly tolerated. Botulinum toxin type A injection into trigger zones has Level B evidence (Bendtsen 2019 review) and is useful as a bridge to surgery or in patients who refuse it. Drug therapy controls pain initially in roughly 80% of patients, but durable response falls over years as the underlying nerve injury progresses and many patients eventually require an interventional procedure. Surgical options are stratified by patient fitness and pathology. Microvascular decompression (the Jannetta procedure) achieves 80-90% initial pain relief in classical trigeminal neuralgia with documented neurovascular compression and roughly 70% durable relief at 10 years; it is the only treatment that addresses the cause rather than the symptom. Percutaneous procedures — glycerol rhizotomy, balloon compression, and radiofrequency thermocoagulation of the Gasserian ganglion — offer high initial response rates (around 90%) with shorter recovery but higher recurrence and sensory side effects. Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife or LINAC) delivers focused radiation to the trigeminal root and produces pain freedom in roughly 70% at 1 year and 50% at 5 years; it is the preferred option in elderly or medically frail patients who cannot tolerate open surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Carbamazepine (100 mg twice daily, titrated to 400-1200 mg per day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy and the only agent with Level A evidence in the AAN/EFNS 2008 guideline. Acts as a use-dependent sodium-channel blocker, stabilizing the demyelinated trigeminal nerve and suppressing ectopic discharges. Monitor sodium, liver function, and CBC; screen HLA-B*1502 in Han Chinese, Thai, and Southeast Asian patients before starting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Numbers needed to treat 1.7-2.6 for adequate pain relief; initial response in roughly 70-80% of patients. Long-term tolerability falls to about 50% over years due to drowsiness, ataxia, hyponatremia, and rash.

medicalTreatments.name: Oxcarbazepine (300 mg twice daily, titrated to 600-1800 mg per day)
medicalTreatments.description: Keto-analogue of carbamazepine with similar mechanism. Often used as first-line in many centers because it is better tolerated with fewer drug interactions; monitor sodium because hyponatremia is more common than with carbamazepine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to carbamazepine in head-to-head and observational studies (Bendtsen 2019 review); pain control in roughly 70% of patients with a better adverse-effect profile.

medicalTreatments.name: Lamotrigine (25 mg daily, slow titration to 200-400 mg per day)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line sodium-channel modulator. Useful as add-on therapy in patients with partial response to carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine. Slow titration over 6-8 weeks is essential to minimize the risk of severe rash including Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in approximately 50-60% of partially-responsive patients in small randomized trials (Zakrzewska 1997).

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen (5 mg three times daily, titrated to 30-80 mg per day)
medicalTreatments.description: GABA-B agonist used as add-on therapy, particularly in patients with multiple-sclerosis-related trigeminal neuralgia or carbamazepine intolerance. Withdraw gradually because abrupt cessation can precipitate seizures and hallucinations.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Useful add-on; monotherapy response rates around 30-50% in small open-label studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (300 mg three times daily, titrated to 1800-3600 mg per day) or pregabalin (75 mg twice daily, up to 600 mg per day)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium-channel α2δ ligands with broad neuropathic-pain activity. Lower-quality evidence than carbamazepine in trigeminal neuralgia specifically, but well-tolerated and useful in older patients or as adjuncts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Partial response in roughly 50% of patients in observational series; better evidence base in other neuropathic pain syndromes.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin type A injection (subcutaneous, into trigger zones)
medicalTreatments.description: Repeat injections every 3-4 months into trigger zones identified by examination. Mechanism involves local sensory nerve inhibition and reduction of peripheral sensitization. Bendtsen 2019 review of randomized data assigned Level B evidence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 50% or more in approximately 60-70% of patients in randomized trials; useful as bridge to surgery or in surgically unfit patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Microvascular decompression (Jannetta procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: A posterior fossa craniectomy in which a Teflon felt pad is placed between the trigeminal root and the offending blood vessel, removing the mechanical irritation. Considered the most effective and durable treatment for classical trigeminal neuralgia with documented neurovascular compression. Best candidates are younger, medically fit patients with classical MRI findings.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial pain freedom in 80-90% of patients; durable pain freedom at 10 years in approximately 70% (Barker 1996 long-term series, Tatli and Sindou subsequent reports). Mortality under 0.3% and major complication rates under 5% in high-volume centers.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous radiofrequency thermocoagulation of the Gasserian ganglion
surgicalOptions.description: Targeted thermal injury delivered via a needle through the foramen ovale under fluoroscopic guidance. Performed under conscious sedation so the operator can confirm correct targeting of the affected division. Suitable for elderly or medically frail patients and as a salvage for recurrence after microvascular decompression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial pain relief in around 90% of patients; pain freedom at 5 years roughly 50%. Sensory loss in the treated division is expected and is part of the mechanism.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous glycerol rhizotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Injection of glycerol into the trigeminal cistern via the foramen ovale. Lower technical demand than thermocoagulation, less precise targeting. Suitable as an alternative percutaneous option.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial pain relief in 80-90%; pain freedom at 5 years approximately 40-50%; recurrence rates higher than with thermocoagulation.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous balloon compression
surgicalOptions.description: Inflation of a Fogarty balloon catheter within Meckel's cave to mechanically compress the trigeminal ganglion. Brief procedure performed under general anesthesia. Useful when the V1 division is involved because it spares the corneal reflex better than radiofrequency.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial pain freedom in 85-90%; 5-year pain freedom in roughly 50-60%.

surgicalOptions.name: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife or LINAC)
surgicalOptions.description: A single high-dose focused radiation treatment (typically 70-90 Gy) delivered to the trigeminal root just proximal to the ganglion. Onset of pain relief is delayed by 4-8 weeks. Preferred option in elderly, anticoagulated, or medically frail patients who cannot undergo open surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain freedom at 1 year approximately 70%; at 5 years approximately 50%. Sensory side effects in roughly 20% of patients, usually mild.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Small randomized studies suggest modest short-term reduction in attack frequency. Adjunctive only; should never replace evidence-based pharmacotherapy for an active disorder of this severity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy and pain-coping psychology
alternativeTreatments.description: Reduces the impact of pain on mood, sleep, and function. Particularly important given the elevated rates of depression and suicidality in untreated trigeminal neuralgia.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurologist should be involved at diagnosis to confirm the ICHD-3 criteria, coordinate the MRI workup, and initiate or supervise carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine titration. Refer to neurosurgery when pain is uncontrolled on adequate medical therapy, when significant adverse effects prevent therapeutic doses, when MRI shows clear neurovascular compression in a patient who is a surgical candidate, or when MRI identifies a structural lesion requiring intervention.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in neurology with subspecialty interest in headache or facial pain. For surgical management, choose a neurosurgical center with a documented annual volume of at least 20 microvascular decompressions or stereotactic radiosurgery cases, published outcomes data, and access to high-resolution MRI with CISS/FIESTA imaging. Continuity of care matters — diagnosis is clinical and follow-up is multi-year.
preventionStrategies: Trigeminal neuralgia cannot be primarily prevented because the underlying neurovascular or structural mechanisms cannot be predicted or modified before symptom onset
Control blood pressure long-term to limit arterial elongation and tortuosity, which contribute to root compression
Adhere to disease-modifying therapy in multiple sclerosis to reduce the formation of new pontine plaques that can cause secondary trigeminal neuralgia
Once diagnosed, prevent attack escalation by adhering to medication, avoiding sudden drug discontinuation, and identifying personal triggers
Continue dental care under the supervision of a dentist familiar with trigeminal neuralgia — local anesthetic blocks can be planned to avoid trigger zones
lifestyleModifications: Eat soft, room-temperature foods during active disease, cut food into small pieces, and use a straw if oral movement provokes attacks
Adopt a daily oral hygiene routine using a soft-bristled brush and lukewarm water; consider a water flosser if mechanical brushing triggers pain
Shield the face from cold wind and air-conditioning drafts with a scarf or shawl during flare periods
Keep a pain diary recording timing, triggers, and medication doses — patterns guide titration and surgical timing decisions
Schedule activities and meals during periods of best pain control, typically after the day's first medication doses take effect
Address depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbance with a clinician early; these magnify pain experience and disability
Avoid alcohol during initial carbamazepine titration because of additive sedative effects

recommended: Soft foods such as well-cooked vegetables, yogurt, oatmeal, eggs, fish, and tender meats that require minimal chewing
Cool or room-temperature drinks taken through a straw to bypass trigger zones around the lips and cheek
Smoothies and pureed soups during severe flares to maintain calorie and protein intake
Adequate hydration with water at neutral temperature, monitoring sodium status if on carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine
Small frequent meals rather than large meals that require prolonged chewing
avoid: Very hot or very cold foods and drinks if temperature is a personal trigger
Hard, crunchy, or sticky foods such as raw apples, crusty bread, nuts, and chewy candies during flares
Excessive caffeine in the evening, which can disrupt the sleep needed for pain recovery
Alcohol while titrating sodium-channel blockers because of additive central nervous system depression
Foods that consistently trigger personal attacks, identified through diary tracking

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic exercise such as stationary cycling, walking, and swimming is safe and recommended during periods of pain control. Avoid activities that provoke trigger zones — wind on the face during outdoor cycling, cold-water swimming, or contact sports with facial impact. Resume normal exertion as pain allows; physical activity reduces depression and improves general pain tolerance.
dailyManagement: Take carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine at the same times each day, with food, to maintain steady plasma levels
Monitor for warning signs of severe drug reaction in the first 8 weeks — new rash, mouth ulcers, fever, swollen lymph nodes — and seek urgent review if these occur
Recheck sodium and full blood count at 2 weeks, 1 month, and every 3-6 months while on chronic carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine
Carry a brief written summary of diagnosis, MRI findings, current medications, and emergency contacts in case of unexpected severe flare
Plan a rescue strategy for breakthrough pain in discussion with the prescriber — short-acting baclofen, additional carbamazepine dose, or scheduled botulinum toxin top-up
prognosis: The natural course of trigeminal neuralgia is relapsing and remitting, with progressively shorter pain-free intervals over years. With adequate carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, around 70-80% of patients achieve initial pain control, but long-term tolerability falls to roughly 50% over time as nerve injury advances and drug doses required to maintain response approach the tolerability ceiling. Microvascular decompression offers the best chance of long-term cure in classical neurovascular-compression disease, with approximately 80-90% pain free at one year and 70% at 10 years. Percutaneous procedures and stereotactic radiosurgery offer high initial relief with recurrence rates that rise after 3-5 years and the possibility of repeat treatment. Secondary trigeminal neuralgia from multiple sclerosis is more refractory, with higher relapse rates after every modality. Untreated, the disorder carries substantial morbidity from malnutrition, weight loss, depression, social isolation, and elevated suicide risk; mortality from the disease itself is low, but quality-of-life impairment is among the worst measured in any chronic pain condition.
recoveryTimeline: Pharmacological response to carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine typically appears within 24-72 hours of reaching an adequate dose, with full effect over 1-2 weeks of stable dosing. Microvascular decompression patients leave hospital in 3-5 days, return to light activity in 2-4 weeks, and reach maximal recovery by 3 months; pain relief is usually immediate after surgery. Percutaneous procedures provide pain relief within 24-48 hours and recovery to normal activity within 1-2 weeks. Stereotactic radiosurgery is delayed in effect, with pain relief emerging 4-8 weeks after treatment and continuing to develop for up to 6 months.
complications: Depression and suicidal ideation — among the highest rates of any chronic pain disorder; routine mental-health screening is essential at every visit
Weight loss and malnutrition from avoidance of eating, drinking, and oral hygiene during flare periods
Dental complications from misdiagnosis — repeated unnecessary extractions and root canals are common before correct diagnosis
Severe cutaneous drug reactions including Stevens-Johnson syndrome and DRESS with carbamazepine or lamotrigine, especially in HLA-B*1502 carriers
Hyponatremia from carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, with risk of confusion, falls, and seizures if sodium drops below 125 mmol/L
Surgical complications of microvascular decompression — cerebrospinal fluid leak, hearing loss, facial weakness, cerebellar injury — in roughly 1-5% of cases
Persistent facial sensory loss, anesthesia dolorosa, or corneal anesthesia after ablative procedures or radiosurgery

supportResources.name: Facial Pain Association
supportResources.description: US-based patient association with educational materials, peer support, and clinician finder for trigeminal neuralgia and related disorders.

supportResources.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia Association UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing patient information, helpline support, and advocacy for facial pain disorders.

supportResources.name: International Headache Society — ICHD-3 Resources
supportResources.description: Authoritative diagnostic criteria from the international body that defines headache and facial pain disorders.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cluster Headache
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cluster headache produces severe unilateral pain centered around the eye (V1 territory) lasting 15-180 minutes per attack, with autonomic features such as tearing, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion, and ptosis. Attacks are longer than trigeminal neuralgia paroxysms and are not trigger-evoked by light touch. Trigeminal neuralgia more often involves V2 or V3, lasts seconds, and has no autonomic features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Postherpetic Trigeminal Neuralgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Postherpetic trigeminal neuralgia follows herpes zoster ophthalmicus and produces continuous burning or aching pain with allodynia in the affected division (most often V1), often with residual scarring or hypopigmentation along the dermatome. Classical trigeminal neuralgia gives brief electric-shock paroxysms with normal skin and preserved sensation between attacks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dental Abscess and Odontogenic Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dental pain is usually continuous, throbbing, worse with hot or cold stimuli on the tooth, and localized to the offending tooth or surrounding gum. Examination shows a specific tender tooth, percussion pain, and often visible swelling or pus. Trigeminal neuralgia attacks are brief, lancinating, triggered by light touch rather than hot or cold, and have no dental findings on examination.

confusedWithConditions.name: Temporomandibular Joint Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TMJ disorder produces dull, aching pain over the joint or chewing muscles, worsened by jaw movement, often with clicking, locking, or restricted opening. Pain is mechanical and continuous rather than paroxysmal, and is not triggered by light touch to the face.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migraine attacks last 4-72 hours with throbbing pain, nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia, often preceded by aura. The pain is not the brief lancinating paroxysm of trigeminal neuralgia and is not triggered by innocuous facial stimuli.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: MS plaques in the pontine trigeminal pathway cause secondary trigeminal neuralgia in roughly 2-5% of MS patients, often younger and more frequently bilateral.

relatedConditions.name: Postherpetic Trigeminal Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: A separate neuropathic facial pain syndrome that follows herpes zoster ophthalmicus and enters the same differential at first presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Cluster Headache
relatedConditions.relevance: Trigeminal autonomic cephalgia in the same trigeminal distribution that must be distinguished from V1 trigeminal neuralgia by duration, autonomic features, and trigger pattern.

relatedConditions.name: Glossopharyngeal Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Analogous paroxysmal neuralgia of the ninth cranial nerve producing throat and ear-canal pain; shares treatment principles including carbamazepine and microvascular decompression.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently appears in the same differential for unilateral facial and headache pain; can coexist and complicate diagnostic clarity.

faqs.question: What does trigeminal neuralgia feel like?
faqs.answer: Patients describe trigeminal neuralgia as sudden electric-shock-like or stabbing pain on one side of the face, lasting seconds to two minutes per attack. The pain is usually in the cheek, upper or lower jaw, and is triggered by light touch, chewing, brushing the teeth, talking, or a breeze. Many people describe it as the most severe pain they have experienced.

faqs.question: What triggers a trigeminal neuralgia attack?
faqs.answer: Common triggers are light touch to the face, chewing, brushing the teeth, shaving, washing the face, talking, smiling, and a cool draft of air across the cheek. Triggers are typically innocuous stimuli that would not be painful in a healthy person, and trigger zones are usually small areas around the nasolabial fold, upper lip, or chin.

faqs.question: How is trigeminal neuralgia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Trigeminal neuralgia is diagnosed clinically using the International Classification of Headache Disorders criteria — recurrent unilateral brief electric-shock-like facial pain in trigeminal distribution triggered by innocuous stimuli. High-resolution MRI of the brain with dedicated trigeminal nerve sequences is required in every patient to identify neurovascular compression and to exclude multiple sclerosis or a tumor.

faqs.question: Why do I need an MRI for trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: MRI identifies neurovascular compression at the trigeminal root, which supports a classical diagnosis and guides surgical planning. It also detects multiple sclerosis plaques and cerebellopontine angle tumors that cause secondary trigeminal neuralgia. The AAN/EFNS 2008 guideline recommends MRI for every patient regardless of how typical the clinical picture appears.

faqs.question: What is the first-line medication for trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Carbamazepine is the first-line medication and the only drug with Level A evidence in international guidelines. Treatment is usually started at 100 mg twice daily and increased every 3-7 days to clinical effect, with most patients responding between 400 and 1200 mg per day in divided doses. Reported numbers needed to treat are 1.7-2.6.

faqs.question: Is oxcarbazepine better than carbamazepine?
faqs.answer: Oxcarbazepine has comparable efficacy to carbamazepine and is often better tolerated, with fewer drug interactions and less sedation. The trade-off is a higher rate of hyponatremia, which needs sodium monitoring. Many specialists now use oxcarbazepine as first-line for new patients, particularly older adults on multiple other medications.

faqs.question: What is microvascular decompression?
faqs.answer: Microvascular decompression is a neurosurgical procedure that places a small Teflon felt pad between the trigeminal nerve and the blood vessel pressing on it. The operation addresses the cause rather than the symptom. Initial pain relief is achieved in 80-90% of patients with classical disease, and roughly 70% remain pain free 10 years later.

faqs.question: How does Gamma Knife treat trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Gamma Knife stereotactic radiosurgery delivers a single high-dose focused radiation beam to the trigeminal nerve root just proximal to the ganglion. The procedure is non-invasive and takes a few hours. Pain relief is delayed, emerging over 4-8 weeks. Roughly 70% of patients are pain free at one year and 50% at five years; it is preferred for older or medically frail patients.

faqs.question: Can trigeminal neuralgia be cured?
faqs.answer: Microvascular decompression cures the underlying cause in classical trigeminal neuralgia with documented neurovascular compression, with around 70% of patients remaining pain free at 10 years without medication. Drug therapy and percutaneous and radiosurgical procedures control pain but do not address the cause, and recurrence is more common.

faqs.question: Does multiple sclerosis cause trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. A demyelinating plaque in the pontine trigeminal pathway can produce trigeminal neuralgia. Around 2-5% of multiple sclerosis patients develop the condition during their illness, often at younger ages and more frequently bilaterally than classical neurovascular-compression disease.

faqs.question: Is trigeminal neuralgia the same as a migraine?
faqs.answer: No. Migraine attacks last 4-72 hours with throbbing pain, nausea, light and sound sensitivity, and sometimes aura. Trigeminal neuralgia attacks last seconds to two minutes, are electric-shock-like, are triggered by innocuous facial stimuli, and have no nausea or aura. The two are distinct conditions managed differently.

faqs.question: Why is trigeminal neuralgia mistaken for a dental problem?
faqs.answer: The pain often radiates into the upper or lower jaw and teeth, and patients commonly present first to a dentist. Repeated dental procedures, including root canals and extractions, fail to relieve the pain because the source is the trigeminal nerve, not the teeth. Persistent facial pain after adequate dental treatment should prompt a neurology referral.

faqs.question: Can young people get trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Peak onset is between ages 50 and 70, and the condition is uncommon under 40. When trigeminal neuralgia appears in a younger person, especially bilaterally, multiple sclerosis or another structural cause should be sought with MRI. Pediatric presentations are exceptional and almost always have an underlying lesion.

faqs.question: How long do attacks last?
faqs.answer: Individual paroxysms last from a fraction of a second up to two minutes. Attacks can occur in clusters lasting hours, separated by pain-free intervals of minutes to weeks. The brief electric-shock quality and rapid termination distinguish trigeminal neuralgia from migraine, cluster headache, and dental pain.

faqs.question: What is the difference between classical and secondary trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Classical trigeminal neuralgia is caused by neurovascular compression at the trigeminal root with visible morphological change on MRI. Secondary trigeminal neuralgia is caused by an identifiable underlying disorder such as multiple sclerosis, a cerebellopontine angle tumor, or a vascular malformation. The two differ in age of onset, response to treatment, and surgical options.

faqs.question: Can I have trigeminal neuralgia on both sides of the face?
faqs.answer: Bilateral trigeminal neuralgia is rare overall (under 5%) and raises suspicion of a secondary cause, particularly multiple sclerosis. Even when both sides are affected, attacks usually do not occur simultaneously. Bilateral disease should prompt detailed MRI assessment.

metaDescription: Trigeminal neuralgia: brief electric-shock facial pain triggered by light touch. Symptoms, MRI workup, carbamazepine, and surgery reviewed by our medical board.
